_title: BS 7671:2018+A2:2022 Regulation 443 — Protection Against Transient Overvoltages
_note: AMD 2 (2022) made SPD provision effectively mandatory for most commercial installations. Risk assessment is required to determine SPD type and location.

principle.purpose: Surge Protective Devices (SPDs) divert transient overvoltages (lightning, switching transients, indirect strikes) to earth before they damage equipment connected to the installation.
principle.scope: Transient overvoltages of atmospheric origin (lightning) and switching overvoltages. NOT power frequency overvoltages (those covered by Reg 442).

regulatory_requirement.amd2_clarification: Reg 443.4 — Protection against transient overvoltages of atmospheric origin or due to switching shall be provided where the consequence caused by overvoltage affects: a) serious injury or loss of human life; b) interruption of public services or damage to cultural heritage; c) interruption of commercial or industrial activity; d) affects a large number of co-located individuals.
regulatory_requirement.in_practice: Most commercial and public buildings — SPD required. Domestic installations — SPD required unless risk assessment justifies otherwise (CRL ≤ 1000 calculation per Reg 443.5).
regulatory_requirement.risk_assessment_method: Two routes accepted by BS 7671: (1) Calculated Risk Level (CRL) per Reg 443.5 formula — if CRL > 1000, SPD required. (2) Conservative approach — install SPD by default.
exceptions: Domestic where CRL calculation gives ≤ 1000 AND no critical equipment.
Single dwelling unit in a multi-occupancy building where SPD is fitted at the building origin.
Where the consequence of overvoltage damage is acceptable to the client (rare in practice — document in design).

type_1.tested_with: 10/350 µs waveform — simulates direct lightning strike
use_cases: Buildings with external Lightning Protection System (LPS) — REQUIRED at the origin
Buildings on hilltops or otherwise exposed locations
Installations with high lightning exposure (per BS EN 62305 risk assessment)
type_1.location: At the origin of the installation (main switchboard, immediately downstream of the main switch). Must be installed BEFORE any other equipment.

type_2.tested_with: 8/20 µs waveform — simulates induced surges from nearby strikes or switching
type_2.nominal_discharge_current_In: Typically 20 kA per pole, 40 kA per pole for larger
use_cases: All commercial main switchboards (default requirement post-AMD 2)
Where Type 1 is not required (no LPS, urban location)
type_2.location: Main switchboard. In larger buildings, also at sub-distribution boards.

type_3.tested_with: Combination wave 1.2/50 µs voltage + 8/20 µs current
use_cases: Local protection for sensitive equipment (PCs, servers, medical equipment, control systems)
AV equipment, broadcast equipment, lab instrumentation
Installed within 10m of the protected equipment
type_3.location: Final sub-distribution boards or socket-outlet adjacent. Always downstream of a Type 2.
type_3.voltage_protection_level_Up: Typically ≤ 1.5 kV (some Type 3 down to ≤ 0.5 kV)
type_3.note: Type 3 must NEVER be used alone. Always coordinate with upstream Type 2.

combined_type_1_2.description: Single device tested for both Type 1 and Type 2 waveforms. Common in modern installations where Type 1 is required.
combined_type_1_2.use_case: Installations with LPS — provides Type 1 protection at origin plus Type 2 performance downstream in one device.

coordination.principle: Cascaded SPDs must be coordinated so the upstream device clamps the surge before the downstream device is overwhelmed.
rules: Type 1 → Type 2: at least 10m of cable between them OR specified by manufacturer as 'pre-coordinated set'
Type 2 → Type 3: at least 10m of cable between them OR manufacturer-coordinated set
If physical distance is insufficient: use a decoupling inductor (typically 15 µH per pole) between devices
Never install Type 3 directly downstream of Type 1 without Type 2 in between

impulse_withstand_categories._basis: Equipment is categorised by its rated impulse voltage withstand Uw — the maximum surge it can survive without damage. SPDs must reduce Up below the equipment's category.

category_IV.description: Equipment at or near the origin of the installation — electricity meters, main switches, primary overcurrent protection. Heavy industrial duty.

category_III.description: Equipment in the fixed installation — distribution boards, MCCBs, fixed wiring, fixed loads (motors, heaters, fixed lighting).

category_II.description: Equipment intended for connection to the fixed installation — appliances, portable tools, most consumer equipment.

category_I.description: Equipment with reduced impulse withstand — electronic equipment, computers, sensitive instruments. Requires upstream SPD to bring surges below 1.5 kV.

Uc.name: Maximum continuous operating voltage
Uc.selection_rule: Uc ≥ 1.1 × U0 (i.e. ≥ 253V for 230V single-phase, ≥ 440V for 400V three-phase)

Up.name: Voltage protection level
Up.selection_rule: Up ≤ Uw of the equipment being protected. For category III equipment (most fixed wiring): Up ≤ 2.5 kV typically.

Iimp.name: Impulse current (Type 1 only)
Iimp.selection_rule: Per BS EN 62305 lightning risk assessment. Typical 12.5 kA per pole.

In.name: Nominal discharge current (Type 2/3)
In.selection_rule: ≥ 5 kA per pole minimum. 20 kA per pole common for main boards.

Imax.name: Maximum discharge current (Type 2/3)
Imax.selection_rule: Indicates how many strikes the device can take. Higher = longer service life.

connecting_lead_length.rule: Total connecting lead length (line + earth) shall not exceed 0.5m
connecting_lead_length.rationale: Long leads add inductance, which significantly increases Up at the protected equipment. The SPD becomes ineffective.
connecting_lead_length.best_practice: Use the shortest possible leads. Bond directly to MET (Main Earthing Terminal) or busbar where possible.

overcurrent_protection.rule: SPDs require dedicated overcurrent protection per the manufacturer's instructions. Typical: 125A or 160A gG fuses for Type 1+2 at origin.
overcurrent_protection.purpose: Disconnects the SPD if it fails short-circuit (end-of-life mode). Prevents fire.

status_indication.rule: SPDs shall provide local visual indication of operational status (green = healthy, red = failed).
status_indication.additional: For unmanned plant rooms, remote indication to BMS or alarm panel recommended.

tn_s_tn_c_s_arrangement.description: Connect L1, L2, L3 to PE via SPD modules. Connect N to PE via separate N-PE module if required (depends on the SPD topology — Type 3+1 or 4+0 configuration).
tn_s_tn_c_s_arrangement.common_config: 4+0 for TN-S (4 modules: L1-PE, L2-PE, L3-PE, N-PE). 3+1 for TT (3 modules L-N + 1 module N-PE).

tt_arrangement.description: Specific 3+1 configuration. SPD must be installed BEFORE the RCD to avoid earth fault current passing through the RCD during surge.
tt_arrangement.rcd_type: Use Type S (selective) RCD downstream of SPD to ensure SPD operation does not trip the RCD.

worked_example.description: 4-storey commercial office, urban location, no external LPS, single 1600A TN-C-S supply
worked_example.lps_status: No LPS present per BS EN 62305 risk assessment

device_specification.overcurrent_protection: 125A gG fuse per pole (per manufacturer requirement)

worked_example.location: Immediately downstream of the main incoming switch at the MSB. Maximum 0.5m connecting leads to phase busbars and to MET.
worked_example.downstream_strategy: Additional Type 2 SPDs at each floor SDB for sensitive IT loads. Type 3 at server room PDU as final stage.

common_pitfalls: No SPD where AMD 2 requires it — non-compliant since September 2022.
Type 1 specified without an LPS — Type 2 would suffice and is cheaper.
Type 3 used as sole protection — incorrect, must be coordinated with upstream Type 2.
No status indication — failed SPD goes unnoticed until next strike.
Missing dedicated overcurrent protection — fire risk on end-of-life failure.
Forgetting separate N-PE module on TT system.
